headline: Summit season meets the ground truth: NATO leaders gather in Ankara to fund a war that just showed Ukraine can't defend its own skies, France's top court decides today whether Marine Le Pen can run for president, Iran readies Khamenei's burial with its new leader still unseen — while India turns diplomacy into hardware, sealing a BrahMos-missile pact in Jakarta and watching five of its states cross a World Bank income line.
the_big_picture: The throughline today is decision day — several stories that had been building all week now reach the moment where something is actually settled. NATO's 32 leaders, Donald Trump among them, open a summit in Ankara to enforce last year's promise to spend 5% of GDP on defence and to pledge roughly €70bn to Ukraine — an argument made urgent by Monday's record barrage on Kyiv, in which every Russian ballistic missile got through because Ukraine has almost run out of interceptors. In Paris a court rules from 1:30pm on whether the far right's frontrunner can even stand in 2027. In Iran, a nation of 90 million prepares to bury Ayatollah Khamenei in Mashhad on Wednesday while the son who replaced him has still not been seen. Underneath the geopolitics, the mood in markets is quietly risk-on: Wall Street reopened after its long weekend to a record Dow and 'revived AI optimism', the fear gauge sank, gold softened and oil ticked up off deeply oversold lows. And India spent the day doing what middle powers do — converting relationships into concrete things: Modi signed a defence pact to sell Indonesia BrahMos supersonic missiles and collected its highest civilian honour, five Indian states were revealed to have crossed the World Bank's 'upper-middle-income' threshold, and the market logged a fifth straight up-day, this time led by the very IT firms everyone fears AI will disrupt, ahead of TCS's results on Thursday. It is a day of ledgers being closed — on a war's funding, a politician's future, a leader's life, and a country's rung on the income ladder.

stories.title: NATO summit opens in Ankara — Trump demands the money, Ukraine begs for interceptors after its worst night
key_points: 32 NATO leaders meet in Ankara today and tomorrow (7–8 July)
Trump is there to enforce last year's pledge to spend 5% of GDP on defence
Allies set to pledge ~€70bn ($80bn) in 2026 military aid to Ukraine
Follows Monday's strike that killed 23 as every ballistic missile got through
stories.background: NATO is the 32-nation military alliance that binds the US, Canada and most of Europe under a promise of mutual defence. At last year's summit Trump extracted a pledge that members would raise defence spending toward 5% of GDP (the size of their economies) — a huge jump. This year's summit, in Turkey's capital Ankara, is where he tries to make them honour it. It convenes days after Russia's heaviest barrage of the war exposed that Ukraine's US-made Patriot air-defence systems — the only weapon that can reliably stop Russia's fastest 'ballistic' missiles — have almost run out of interceptor missiles.
stories.what_happened: Leaders gather in Ankara on 7–8 July with three stated priorities from Secretary-General Mark Rutte: raise allied defence investment, expand Europe's own arms production, and keep supporting Ukraine. Members are expected to pledge around €70bn ($80bn) in military aid to Kyiv for 2026 and 'at least equivalent' amounts in 2027, and to sign billions in new defence contracts. Trump is due to meet Ukraine's President Zelensky on Wednesday. The backdrop is grim: Monday's assault on the Kyiv region killed 23 people, and Ukraine's air force said all 29 Russian ballistic missiles reached their targets — a near-total interception failure blamed on the interceptor shortage.
stories.why_it_matters: This is where words become budgets. Ukraine's ability to keep defending its cities — and therefore its leverage in any peace talks Trump is pushing — depends directly on whether this summit unlocks more Patriot interceptors and faster production. The 5%-spending fight also reshapes Europe: hundreds of billions redirected to defence means less for other priorities, and a continent rearming for a world where it can no longer assume unlimited US cover.
stories.ripple_effects: Concrete aid and interceptor commitments would steady Ukraine's air defence; a summit that dissolves into a spending row would embolden Moscow. Big defence-contract announcements lift European and US arms makers. An escalating war keeps a latent 'war premium' in crude oil and European natural gas even as prices otherwise fall — a standing risk for import-dependent economies like India. And every strike on a capital ratchets up Europe's threat perception and its willingness to spend.
stories.why_now: The summit was scheduled for today, but Monday's barrage and the interceptor shortage have turned an annual budget meeting into an emergency about whether Ukraine can be kept in the fight at all.
stories.watch_next: Whether Ankara produces firm Patriot-interceptor commitments and the €70bn aid pledge in writing — and what comes out of Wednesday's Trump–Zelensky meeting.

key_terms.definition: A defence-spending target measured against the size of a country's economy; moving from ~2% to 5% is an enormous increase in military budgets.
The missile a US-made Patriot battery fires to destroy an incoming ballistic missile; each is costly and made in limited numbers, so stockpiles can run dry.

stories.the_lesson: Alliances are tested not by their promises but by their invoices — a summit reveals how much collective defence a group will actually pay for, and on the battlefield the side that can still shoot down incoming missiles keeps its bargaining power.
stories.market_link: Supportive for defence contractors; escalation keeps a war premium latent in crude (WTI ~$69) and European gas, while a credible aid-and-diplomacy track would ease it.

thread.previously: Yesterday we reported Russia's heaviest barrage on Kyiv — 68 missiles and 351 drones — which exposed that Ukraine has nearly exhausted its Patriot interceptors, and flagged today's NATO summit in Ankara as the venue for a fight over who pays for that defence.
thread.changed: The summit is now underway: the death toll from Monday's strike rose to 23, and NATO is expected to formalise a ~€70bn Ukraine aid pledge and Trump's 5%-spending enforcement, with a Trump–Zelensky meeting set for Wednesday.

stories.title: Modi seals a BrahMos-missile pact in Jakarta and collects Indonesia's highest honour
key_points: India and Indonesia sign a pact to supply BrahMos supersonic missiles
Also inked: Astra air-to-air missiles, maritime security, critical minerals
Prabowo confers Indonesia's top award, the Bintang Adipurna, on Modi
Seven-to-eight agreements span defence, health, education and steel
stories.background: India's 'Act East' policy is its long push to deepen trade and security ties across Southeast Asia and the Pacific, partly as a counterweight to China. Indonesia is the region's biggest economy, guards vital sea lanes, and holds roughly a fifth of the world's nickel — a critical battery metal. BrahMos is a supersonic cruise missile jointly built by India and Russia; selling it abroad marks India's shift from arms buyer to arms exporter. This is the first leg of a three-nation tour that next takes Modi to Australia and, for the first Indian PM visit in about 40 years, New Zealand.
stories.what_happened: In Jakarta on 7 July, Modi and President Prabowo Subianto oversaw seven-to-eight agreements, headlined by a pact for India to supply BrahMos missiles to the Indonesian military, plus Astra air-to-air missiles, a maritime-security framework, and cooperation on critical minerals and steel supply chains. Prabowo conferred Indonesia's highest honour, the Bintang Adipurna, on Modi, who said it 'belongs to the crores of Indians.'
stories.why_it_matters: This is diplomacy converted into concrete supply chains and defence exports. A BrahMos sale is a landmark for India's ambition to be a serious arms exporter; securing nickel and steel-chain access feeds its battery and manufacturing goals; and the maritime pact deepens India's Indo-Pacific footprint against China's. For Indonesia, closer ties diversify partners beyond Beijing.
stories.ripple_effects: Deliverables benefit Indian defence manufacturers (BrahMos Aerospace) and companies building EV and steel supply chains that need nickel. A proven export template makes India more attractive to other buyers eyeing BrahMos. Watch the next legs: the India–Australia summit in Melbourne (8–10 July) on defence and critical minerals, then New Zealand.
stories.why_now: The state visit was scheduled for 7–8 July, timed as India accelerates Indo-Pacific deals — its UK trade pact goes live 15 July and a New Zealand FTA is due later this year.
stories.watch_next: Delivery timelines and battery numbers on the BrahMos deal, and the outcomes of the India–Australia annual summit (8–10 July).

key_terms.definition: A supersonic cruise missile jointly developed by India and Russia; India selling it abroad is a milestone in becoming an arms exporter.
Metals like nickel and copper essential for batteries, chips and clean energy, whose supply a few countries dominate.

stories.the_lesson: Middle powers grow influence by weaving overlapping webs — arms, minerals, payments — so no single partner can hold them hostage. Modern diplomacy is measured in signed supply chains as much as in handshakes.
stories.market_link: Watch Indian defence and EV/battery-linked names for summit-driven news; the deal-flow supports broad India sentiment on a risk-on day.

thread.previously: Yesterday we reported Modi landing in Jakarta to a fighter-jet escort, with the agenda sharpening around BrahMos sales, nickel and a UPI–QRIS payments link.
thread.changed: The agreements are now signed — a BrahMos-supply pact plus Astra missiles, maritime security and critical-minerals deals — and Modi was conferred Indonesia's highest civilian honour.

stories.title: France's top court rules today on whether Marine Le Pen can run for president
key_points: Paris Court of Appeal delivers its ruling from 1:30pm today (7 July)
At stake: a five-year ban on holding public office
A ban would knock the poll-leading far-right leader out of 2027
Protege Jordan Bardella, 30, would replace her if she is barred
stories.background: Marine Le Pen has led France's far right for over a decade and tops polls for the 2027 presidential race. In 2025 a court convicted her and other National Rally figures of embezzling European Parliament funds — using EU money meant for parliamentary assistants to pay party staff between 2004 and 2016 — and barred her from public office. She appealed; without a reversal or a shortened ban she cannot run in 2027.
stories.what_happened: The Paris Court of Appeal delivers its verdict from 1:30pm local time today; the reading could take several hours. Prosecutors recommended a four-year sentence (one year in custody, possibly electronically monitored), a €100,000 fine and — the decisive part — a five-year ban on holding public office. The court can uphold that ban, cut it to two years or less (which would expire before the April 2027 first round), or impose none. The sentence will not be enforced immediately.
stories.why_it_matters: One ruling could redraw European politics. If the ban stands, Le Pen's 30-year-old protege Jordan Bardella likely becomes the far-right candidate — popular but far less tested. The case also sharpens a democratic tension seen across the West: courts enforcing the law against politicians versus voters' right to choose whom they want, a clash the far right will frame as 'political justice' either way.
stories.ripple_effects: A ban would fuel 'lawfare' accusations and could rally the base behind Bardella; a soft ruling would instantly make Le Pen the 2027 frontrunner again. Investors track French elections because far-right, high-spending fiscal plans have previously unsettled the bond market — where governments borrow — and pressured the euro.
stories.why_now: The appeals court fixed 7 July as its ruling date back in February; the verdict lands this afternoon.
stories.watch_next: The ruling itself — specifically whether the public-office ban is upheld, shortened to two years or less, or dropped — and how markets and the RN respond.

key_terms.definition: A legal bar on holding or running for public office, sometimes imposed as part of a criminal sentence.
A higher court that re-examines a conviction or sentence and can uphold, change or cancel it.

stories.the_lesson: In modern democracies a courtroom can matter as much as a ballot box — a single ruling can remove a frontrunner and reroute a country's politics, which is why 'political risk' includes judicial calendars.
stories.market_link: French government bonds and the euro are sensitive to any outcome that raises the odds of a far-right presidency in 2027.

thread.previously: Yesterday we said the verdict was hours away, with prosecutors seeking a five-year ban that would end Le Pen's 2027 bid.
thread.changed: It is now ruling day — the court reads its decision from 1:30pm, with the specific options (uphold, shorten to ≤2 years, or drop the ban) now on the table.

stories.title: Five Indian states cross the World Bank's 'upper-middle-income' line — Delhi leads
key_points: Delhi, Karnataka, Telangana, Tamil Nadu and Gujarat clear the bar
Delhi tops at ~$6,217 per person; Gujarat fifth at ~$4,734
The threshold: GNI per head between ~$4,636 and ~$14,375
A sign of how unevenly India's prosperity is spread
stories.background: Each year the World Bank sorts economies by income per person into low, lower-middle, upper-middle and high. India as a whole is still 'lower-middle-income'. But India is vast and unequal, so some states are far richer than the national average. Measuring individual states against the Bank's global benchmark is a way to see which pockets of India have quietly reached the level of far wealthier countries.
stories.what_happened: Analysis using the World Bank's per-capita income benchmark found five Indian states now sit in the 'upper-middle-income' band: Delhi leads at about $6,217 per person, followed by Karnataka ($5,579), Telangana ($5,407), Tamil Nadu ($5,329) and Gujarat ($4,734). The current threshold for the category is a gross national income per head between roughly $4,636 and $14,375; three more states narrowly missed the mark.
stories.why_it_matters: It reframes the 'is India rich or poor?' question: the country's story is really several economies at once. The states that have crossed the line are the ones with strong services, technology, manufacturing and urban hubs (Bengaluru, Hyderabad, Chennai, the industrial west) — evidence that targeted growth engines can pull regions up to global middle-income living standards even while the national average lags.
stories.ripple_effects: For investors and companies, it sharpens where India's consuming class and skilled labour actually are. For policy, it intensifies the debate about regional inequality and how richer states subsidise poorer ones through central taxes. And it is a reminder that national averages hide enormous internal gaps — the same lesson that applies to any large, fast-growing economy.
stories.why_now: The figures follow the World Bank's latest annual income classifications, prompting fresh state-by-state comparisons in Indian media.
stories.watch_next: Whether the three states just below the line cross it next year, and how the ranking feeds India's federal debate over tax-sharing between states.

key_terms.definition: Gross National Income divided by population — a standard measure of average income per person used to compare countries' wealth.
A World Bank band for economies with GNI per head roughly between $4,636 and $14,375 — comfortably above poverty but below rich-country levels.

stories.the_lesson: National averages can mislead — in a large, unequal economy, the real story lives at the sub-national level, where a few high-growth regions can reach rich-world benchmarks while the whole still counts as developing.
stories.market_link: A read on where India's affluent-consumer and skilled-labour clusters sit — relevant for consumer, real-estate and services companies targeting the top-earning states.

stories.title: Iran readies Khamenei's burial in Mashhad — the new Supreme Leader is still a ghost
key_points: Burial set for Wednesday, 9 July, at the Imam Reza shrine in Mashhad
The cortege passes through Qom and Iraq's Karbala first
Successor Mojtaba, made leader in March, has not appeared publicly
Reports say he was wounded — possibly disfigured — in the strike that killed his father
stories.background: Ayatollah Ali Khamenei ruled Iran for over three decades and was killed in a US–Israeli airstrike on 28 February. His son Mojtaba was chosen as Supreme Leader by the Assembly of Experts in March but has not been seen or heard in public since, communicating only in writing. A week-long, multi-city state funeral began on 3 July — the regime's first big set-piece since June's ceasefire — and reached its largest, most vengeful crowds this week, with mourners chanting for revenge on Trump and Israel.
stories.what_happened: Khamenei's body is due to be buried on Wednesday, 9 July, at the revered Imam Reza shrine in Mashhad, Iran's holiest city, after processions through the seminary city of Qom and the Iraqi shrine city of Karbala. Mojtaba has stayed absent throughout; US and other reports say he was 'wounded and likely disfigured' in the February strike — with claims he suffered severe facial and leg injuries — and Iran has not said when he will make his first public appearance as leader.
stories.why_it_matters: A country of 90 million, sitting on some of the world's largest oil and gas reserves, is being led by a man no one has seen, months after a war with the US and Israel. In a system built around one supreme figure, an invisible leader is not a personnel footnote — it is open uncertainty about whether the state can make decisions, and about whether hardliners or pragmatists actually hold the reins.
stories.ripple_effects: Oil traders watch Tehran because Iran controls the Strait of Hormuz, the sea gate for about a fifth of the world's oil. Right now that calm is holding — part of why crude sits in the high-$60s — but any hardline consolidation, a botched succession, or a fresh incident would put a risk premium straight back into oil, hitting import-dependent India first.
stories.why_now: The funeral rites culminate in Wednesday's burial, and the succession question grows sharper with every ceremony Mojtaba misses — including, potentially, his own father's interment.
stories.watch_next: Whether Mojtaba appears or speaks at or after the Mashhad burial (9 July), and whether the funeral's vengeful rhetoric hardens into any action against US or Israeli targets.

key_terms.definition: Iran's highest authority — a cleric who outranks the elected president and controls the military, judiciary and state media.
A narrow sea lane between Iran and Oman carrying roughly a fifth of the world's seaborne oil; there is no easy way around it.

stories.the_lesson: In systems built around one person, a leader's death or disappearance is not just a succession question — it is uncertainty about whether the state can still decide, and markets and rivals price that uncertainty immediately.
stories.market_link: Any sign of chaos or hardline escalation in Tehran would revive a risk premium in oil (WTI ~$69, still technically oversold) — a direct concern for oil-importer India.

thread.previously: Yesterday we reported the third-day funeral crowds swelling and turning openly vengeful while successor Mojtaba failed to appear, ahead of a ~9 July burial in Mashhad.
thread.changed: The burial is now firmly set for Wednesday at Mashhad's Imam Reza shrine, with fresh reporting that Mojtaba was wounded and possibly disfigured in the strike — deepening the mystery over Iran's unseen new leader.

stories.title: India's market logs a fifth straight up-day — this time IT leads, ahead of TCS earnings
key_points: Nifty and Sensex edged higher again in Tuesday trade
Leadership rotated from banks to IT — Infosys +3.5%, TCS +1.9%
TCS opens Q1 (Apr–Jun) earnings season on 9 July
The rupee steadied near ~95.3 per dollar
stories.background: India's benchmarks — the Nifty 50 and the 30-stock Sensex — had risen four straight sessions on falling oil and bank strength. Today a different sector took the lead: information-technology (IT) services firms like Tata Consultancy Services (TCS) and Infosys, which build and run software for global clients and earn most of their money in dollars. That matters because a slightly weaker rupee lifts their earnings, and because their results are about to set the tone for the whole earnings season.
stories.what_happened: Sensex and Nifty traded modestly higher again on Tuesday — the Sensex up a few hundred points, the Nifty holding above ~24,450 — with IT the day's clear leader (Infosys up about 3.5%, TCS about 1.9%, Tech Mahindra also gaining) as oil-and-gas names firmed and power and metal stocks slipped. The rupee steadied around 95.3 per dollar. TCS reports its Q1 FY27 numbers on 9 July and has set a record date for a possible dividend.
stories.why_it_matters: The rotation into IT before TCS reports is the market betting that the sector — under a cloud all year over whether AI will disrupt it — will deliver reassuring numbers and orders. Earnings season is the reality check that decides whether a rally built on cheap oil and domestic buying has a foundation: strong IT results validate firm prices; weak ones expose stretched valuations fast.
stories.ripple_effects: IT is a heavyweight in the indices, so its results swing the whole market. Good numbers and upbeat commentary on AI-era demand would lift sentiment and possibly draw back foreign buyers who have been net sellers; a miss would hit IT hardest and puncture the 'India as a calm harbour' story. A weak-ish rupee is a quiet tailwind for these dollar-earners.
stories.why_now: Investors are positioning two days before TCS opens the April–June results season on 9 July, rotating into the sector that reports first.
stories.watch_next: TCS's Q1 results and management commentary on 9 July, then Infosys and the other IT majors — the read on whether AI is a threat or a demand driver for Indian tech.

key_terms.definition: India's first fiscal quarter — April to June 2026; Indian firms report on an April–March financial year.
When investors shift money from one industry to another — here, from banks to IT — often to position ahead of expected news like earnings.

stories.the_lesson: Markets move ahead of the news, not after it — a rotation into a sector before it reports is investors pricing the result they expect, which is why the actual earnings can disappoint even when a stock has already risen.
stories.market_link: Directly drives TCS, Infosys and the Nifty/Sensex; a weaker rupee helps IT exporters' earnings, while TCS's print sets the tone for India's whole season.

stories.title: US stocks reopen to a record on 'revived AI optimism' — the new Fed chair's first minutes land Wednesday
key_points: Dow closed at a record ~53,056 on 6 July; S&P +0.72%, Nasdaq +1.12%
Tech rallied broadly as AI optimism returned
Wednesday brings the first Fed minutes under chair Kevin Warsh
A cooling jobs market (57k in June) keeps the Fed 'stuck'
stories.background: US markets reopened Monday after the long July-4th weekend into a light data calendar whose main event is Wednesday's minutes — the detailed written record — of the June Federal Reserve meeting, the first under new chair Kevin Warsh. The Fed sets US interest rates to balance jobs against inflation; because the dollar anchors the world, its decisions ripple everywhere. June's jobs report was weak (just 57,000 added, about half the forecast), while inflation stays sticky near 4.2%.
stories.what_happened: On 6 July the Dow Jones Industrial Average closed at a record ~53,056 (+0.29%), the S&P 500 rose 0.72% to ~7,537 and the Nasdaq added 1.12% to ~26,121 as 'revived AI optimism' lifted technology broadly — a reversal of last week's rotation out of pricey AI-chip names, and days after AI giant Alphabet was added to the Dow. Attention now turns to Wednesday's Fed minutes for the first real window into how the Warsh-era Fed reasons.
stories.why_it_matters: Weak hiring plus sticky inflation leaves the Fed stuck: no rate hikes to fear (which is why stocks can rally), but no cuts either. With a new chair and no track record, the minutes carry outsized weight — a hawkish, inflation-first tone would firm the dollar and pressure rate-sensitive assets, while any nod to the cooling jobs market nudges 'no hike' toward 'maybe cuts.'
stories.ripple_effects: For India, a firm dollar keeps the rupee soft near ~95.3 and makes foreign investors cautious, while a slowing US consumer is a slow headwind for Indian IT and exporters. Gold — which pays no interest and shines when rate rises fade — has stayed elevated. And the AI-optimism bounce is a direct read-through to Indian IT sentiment.
stories.why_now: Markets reopened after the holiday and the AI trade snapped back; Wednesday's minutes are the first primary document on the new Fed's thinking ahead of the 29 July rate decision.
stories.watch_next: Wednesday's June Fed minutes, then the 29 July rate decision — and whether the next inflation print revives hike fears or opens the door to cuts.

key_terms.definition: The detailed written record of a Federal Reserve meeting, released weeks later; traders mine it for clues about future rate moves the short statement didn't give.
A central bank keeping interest rates elevated for an extended period because inflation stays stubborn — which tends to keep its currency strong.

stories.the_lesson: When leadership changes at a central bank, markets must re-learn its reaction function from scratch — so early documents and speeches move prices more than usual, because there is no track record to lean on.
stories.market_link: US rate expectations drive the dollar, gold, the rupee and foreign flows into India; revived AI optimism lifts chip and cloud names and, by extension, Indian IT sentiment.

thread.previously: Yesterday we explained June's weak 57,000 payrolls and flagged Wednesday's first Warsh-era Fed minutes, with US markets reopening after the holiday.
thread.changed: The reopen delivered a record Dow and a broad tech rally on 'revived AI optimism', reversing last week's AI-chip selloff — setting up Wednesday's minutes as the next real signal.

stories.title: Typhoon Bavi's aftermath begins on America's Pacific islands — Taiwan is next on the track
key_points: Category-5 Bavi has struck Guam, Rota and the Marianas
Winds near 290 km/h (180 mph), gusts to ~350 km/h
2026's third Cat-5, a pace tied to record-warm seas
Forecast track then points at Taiwan around 11 July
stories.background: Guam and the Northern Mariana Islands are small US territories in the western Pacific, home to about 220,000 people and major American military bases. A Category-5 storm — sustained winds above 157 mph — is the top of the scale and rare as a direct hit. Bavi is already 2026's third Cat-5, a frequency scientists link to unusually warm ocean water, the fuel storms feed on.
stories.what_happened: Bavi made landfall on the islands with winds near 290 km/h (180 mph) and gusts around 350 km/h, hammering Rota and passing close to Guam, Tinian and Saipan after evacuations were ordered. Officials are now assessing severe wind and flood damage across territories already battered earlier this season, and forecasters are tracking the storm westward toward Taiwan around 11 July.
stories.why_it_matters: At Category-5 strength a storm can knock out water, power and communications for weeks on islands that are hard to resupply, and the damage compounds where places are struck twice in a season. The bigger economic tail is the forecast path: Taiwan is the heart of the world's advanced-chip manufacturing, so a strong hit there would be a genuine supply-chain event.
stories.ripple_effects: A key US western-Pacific military hub may be degraded for a time. If Bavi holds strength into Taiwan, chipmakers (TSMC and its customers) and their supply chains become a live watch item. Insurance costs for Pacific territories keep climbing — a quiet tax a warming climate levies on island life.
stories.why_now: Peak Pacific typhoon season plus record-warm sea-surface temperatures — the same signal behind this year's unusual run of top-category storms.
stories.watch_next: Damage and casualty reports from Guam, Rota and Saipan, and whether Bavi keeps its intensity on the forecast path toward Taiwan (~11 July).

key_terms.definition: The top of the hurricane/typhoon scale — sustained winds above 157 mph, capable of catastrophic destruction.
How warm the top layer of the ocean is; warmer water gives storms more energy, making the strongest categories more likely.

stories.the_lesson: Warm oceans are storm fuel — as seas heat, the strongest storms become more common, and their economic bite depends on what lies on the track: a chip-making island in the path turns a weather story into a supply-chain one.
stories.market_link: Limited today, but a Bavi turn into Taiwan (~11 July) would become a watch item for chipmakers (TSMC, Nvidia) and their supply chains.

thread.previously: Yesterday we reported Bavi making landfall on Guam and the Marianas with winds near 290 km/h, aftermath assessment beginning and Taiwan next on the track.
thread.changed: The cleanup and damage assessment are now underway across the islands, and the storm's forecast path continues to point toward Taiwan around 11 July — the chip-supply watch item to track.

stories.title: India opens an anti-dumping probe into cheap Chinese electric tractors
key_points: India investigates whether China is 'dumping' electric tractors below cost
A fresh test of a cautious India–China commercial thaw
Aims to protect India's domestic EV and farm-equipment makers
Adverse findings could bring protective import duties
stories.background: 'Dumping' is when a country's exporters sell goods abroad below their home-market or production cost — often to grab market share and undercut local rivals. Trade law lets the importing country respond with 'anti-dumping duties' (extra taxes) if an investigation confirms it and finds domestic harm. India and China, long-time economic rivals, have been in a cautious thaw; probes like this test how far that goodwill stretches.
stories.what_happened: India's trade authorities have opened an anti-dumping investigation into imports of electric tractors from China, examining whether they are being sold in India at unfairly low prices that injure local manufacturers. If the probe finds dumping and harm, India can impose duties to level the playing field for domestic EV and farm-equipment makers.
stories.why_it_matters: It sits at the crossroads of three big themes: India's push to build its own EV and manufacturing base ('Make in India'), its wariness of a flood of cheap Chinese goods, and the delicate state of India–China relations. How India handles it signals whether the thaw survives contact with hard commercial interests.
stories.ripple_effects: Duties would protect Indian tractor and EV makers but could raise prices for farmers buying electric machinery, and might invite Chinese retaliation — the classic trade-off in protectionism. It also fits a global pattern of countries erecting barriers against subsidised Chinese EVs, from Europe to the US.
stories.why_now: The probe was initiated as cheap Chinese EV exports surge worldwide and India accelerates its own electric-mobility and domestic-manufacturing agenda.
stories.watch_next: The investigation's preliminary findings and whether India imposes provisional anti-dumping duties — and any Chinese response.

key_terms.definition: Selling exports below home-market or production cost to grab share and undercut local producers.
An extra import tax a country imposes to offset unfairly cheap foreign goods and protect its own industry.

stories.the_lesson: Trade openness and industrial ambition constantly pull against each other — cheap imports help buyers but can hollow out a nascent domestic industry, so governments reach for anti-dumping duties to buy their own manufacturers time.
stories.market_link: Relevant for Indian tractor and EV/farm-equipment makers; a marker for the broader India–China trade relationship.

stories.title: Europe's record heat turns deadly and unequal — wildfires force evacuations in France
key_points: Germany hit 41.7C on Sunday; a fresh UK/EU heatwave builds
Thousands evacuated from a wildfire in southwest France
Heat hits the poorest hardest — those with no way to cool off
France logged 2,025 excess deaths at its last heatwave peak
stories.background: Europe historically lived without air conditioning, and its grids, homes and hospitals were built for milder summers. That assumption is breaking: June temperature records were smashed across the continent, and a warming climate is compressing once-a-decade extremes into back-to-back weeks. Heat is now Europe's deadliest weather — France recorded 2,025 'excess deaths' (deaths above the normal expected number) at its last heatwave peak.
stories.what_happened: Germany hit an unprecedented 41.7C in Brandenburg on Sunday as another heatwave built, and the UK issued a week-long health alert with temperatures heading for 34C. In southwest France, a wildfire forced thousands to evacuate. Reporting from Paris underlined how unevenly the danger falls — people in low-income areas often have 'nothing to cool off with' while wealthier households buy air conditioning.
stories.why_it_matters: Extreme heat is a silent killer and an economic drag — it endangers health, buckles infrastructure, cuts outdoor-work productivity and spikes electricity demand exactly when supply is tightest. And it lands unequally: the poorest, the elderly and outdoor workers bear the worst, turning a weather event into a social-justice one.
stories.ripple_effects: Surging cooling demand raises power prices and blackout risk, drives record imports of (mostly Chinese-made) air conditioners — complicating EU–China trade talks — and strengthens the case for grid upgrades and more generation. Wildfires add insurance losses and, in farming regions, threaten crops.
stories.why_now: Peak Northern-Hemisphere summer amplified by a warming climate has produced back-to-back record heat events across Europe within weeks.
stories.watch_next: Whether this heatwave breaks national July records, how grids cope with record cooling demand, and the spread of the French wildfires.

key_terms.definition: Deaths above the normal expected number for a period — the standard way to count a heatwave's true toll, since heat rarely appears on a death certificate.
When electricity demand nears the limit of what the power system can supply, raising blackout risk and prices.

stories.the_lesson: Climate change shows up first as an infrastructure-and-inequality bill — societies built for yesterday's weather must retrofit grids and homes, and who can afford to adapt, and who can't, becomes a political fight.
stories.market_link: Heat lifts power prices and utility load, boosts cooling-appliance makers, and adds a weather premium to European energy markets.

thread.previously: Yesterday we covered thousands evacuated from a southwest-France wildfire and a fresh UK health alert, with Germany's record 41.7C and France's 2,025 excess deaths.
thread.changed: The heatwave is still building across the UK and continent, keeping grids, wildfires and the unequal toll of extreme heat in focus.

also_notable.title: Hamas hands Gaza's civil administration to a technocratic committee — but disarmament stays stuck
also_notable.why: A real step in the US-backed peace plan, though the hard core (Hamas giving up weapons, Israel withdrawing) remains frozen.

also_notable.title: TCS opens India's Q1 earnings season on 9 July — with a possible dividend
also_notable.why: The first big print of the season and the market's key read on whether AI is a threat or a demand driver for Indian IT.

also_notable.title: Trump confirms he asked FIFA to review a US striker's World Cup ban
also_notable.why: An extraordinary political intervention in sport that drew a 'red line' rebuke from UEFA.

also_notable.title: India–UK free-trade pact (CETA) takes effect 15 July — 99% of Indian exports go duty-free
also_notable.why: One week out: a template for a more trade-open India, benefiting textiles, leather, gems and engineering exporters.

disclaimer: Decoded from public reporting for general understanding. Verify before relying on any single claim; details evolve.
